title: Fever with a red, painful leg

chart.oneLiner: 60M presents with 2 days of an expanding red, warm, painful area on the lower left leg, with fever.

opening: My lower leg has gone red, hot, and painful, and it's been spreading.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 60-year-old man, uncomfortable, limping. Good historian.

HPI: 2 days of an expanding area of redness, warmth, swelling, and pain over the left shin, with fever. A small cut/scratch from gardening a few days ago served as a portal of entry (only if asked). Has athlete's foot between the toes (only if asked/examined). NO severe pain out of proportion, NO skin necrosis/bullae, NO crepitus, NO rapidly advancing margins by the hour (these would suggest necrotizing infection).
PMH: type 2 diabetes, venous insufficiency. MEDS: metformin. SOCIAL: nonsmoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: comfortable except for the leg.
- Vitals: BP 132/80, HR 96, RR 16, 38.3°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Left lower leg: warm, erythematous, tender area with somewhat ill-defined borders and mild swelling; tender to touch; NO fluctuance/abscess, NO crepitus, NO bullae or skin necrosis; calf soft, NO cord/severe calf tenderness (helps distinguish DVT).
- Tinea/maceration between the toes if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the gardening cut or athlete's foot unless asked about skin breaks/portal of entry or foot problems.
- The absence of necrotizing features and the portal of entry are revealed only if examined/asked.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Lactate: 1.5 — normal (a high lactate/toxicity would raise concern for necrotizing infection).
labs.BloodCultures: Drawn given fever; low yield in uncomplicated cellulitis.
labs.Imaging: Ultrasound if abscess or DVT suspected — here no drainable collection, no DVT.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus bradycardia ~46 with subtle J-point elevation; otherwise no acute changes.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus bradycardia ~46 with subtle J-point elevation; otherwise no acute changes. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Blunting of the costophrenic angle with a meniscus and homogeneous basal opacity.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Blunting of the costophrenic angle with a meniscus and homogeneous basal opacity. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing pleural effusion meniscus

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Cellulitis (leading,7). NECROTIZING fasciitis (CANT-MISS — exclude,5). Abscess requiring drainage (CANT-MISS,3). Deep vein thrombosis (CANT-MISS to distinguish,4). Stasis dermatitis / contact dermatitis (core,2). Gout/septic arthritis (core,2). Names cellulitis while explicitly screening for necrotizing features and abscess/DVT (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cellulitis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: NECROTIZING fasciitis; Abscess requiring drainage; Deep vein thrombosis. Also reasonable: Stasis dermatitis / contact dermatitis; Gout/septic arthritis.

coreActions.item: Names cellulitis while explicitly screening for necrotizing features and abscess/DVT

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? (Cellulitis is largely a clinical diagnosis.)
steps.rubric: Recognizes cellulitis is mainly CLINICAL; mark the borders to track progression (4). CBC (2). Assess for necrotizing features and abscess at the bedside (4). Ultrasound if abscess/DVT suspected (3). Blood cultures only if systemically ill (2). PENALTY: routine broad imaging/cultures in uncomplicated cellulitis (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP; Lactate; Blood cultures; Imaging.

coreActions.item: Recognizes cellulitis is mainly clinical; mark the borders to track progression
Assess for necrotizing features and abscess at the bedside
Blood cultures only if systemically ill

penalties.item: routine broad imaging/cultures in uncomplicated cellulitis

contraindicatedTests: routine broad imaging/cultures in uncomplicated cellulitis

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus bradycardia (~46) with benign J-point elevation, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: recognizes the slow rate / sinus bradycardia (7); no acute ischemic changes (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: missing the slow rate / over-reading (-2).
commonMistakes: pathologizing benign sinus bradycardia / missing the slow rate
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus bradycardia (~46) with benign J-point elevation; no acute ischemic change.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = pleural effusion (costophrenic-angle blunting with a meniscus). CREDIT: identifies the blunted costophrenic angle with a meniscus (6); names a pleural effusion (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the effusion (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Pleural effusion: costophrenic-angle blunting with a meniscus sign.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Spreading erythema/warmth/tenderness with a portal of entry, no necrotizing features. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Cellulitis (6). Explicitly excludes necrotizing fasciitis (no pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or systemic toxicity) and abscess/DVT (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Cellulitis. Necrotizing fasciitis is argued against by the absence of pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, skin necrosis, and systemic toxicity; no fluctuant abscess and no cord/calf findings to suggest DVT.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH SSTI: antibiotics by purulence – cephalexin/cefazolin nonpurulent, add MRSA coverage if purulent (CANT-MISS,7); incision & drainage of any abscess (CANT-MISS,5); mark borders and elevate limb (3); treat predisposing factor – tinea/edema (3); ~5-day course (2). PENALTY: broad MRSA coverage for simple nonpurulent cellulitis (-2); missing an abscess that needs drainage (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Nonpurulent cellulitis: cover streptococci/MSSA – cephalexin or cefazolin (IV if systemic). Purulent cellulitis/abscess: incision & drainage PLUS MRSA coverage (TMP-SMX or doxycycline PO; vancomycin IV if severe). Elevate and mark the borders; treat the predisposing factor (tinea, edema). Antibiotics for ~5 days. Blood cultures only if systemic toxicity/immunocompromise.

Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 60M presents with 2 days of an expanding red, warm, painful area on the lower left leg, with fever. The diagnosis is Cellulitis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Left lower leg: warm, erythematous, tender area with somewhat ill-defined borders and mild swelling; tender to touch; NO fluctuance/abscess, NO crepitus, NO bullae or skin necrosis; calf soft, NO cord/severe calf tenderness (helps distinguish DVT).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Cellulitis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Cellulitis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus bradycardia (~46) with benign J-point elevation; no acute ischemic changes.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
